title: INTERDEPARTMENTAL JURISDICTION AUDIT
subtitle: APD Compliance Review, Conducted Prior to Full License Ratification
author: Joint Compliance Review Board (RAISA / ISD / IA)
summary: The compliance review conducted before the Foundation extended APD its exclusive license, addressing jurisdictional overlap with RAISA, Internal Security, and the Intelligence Agency.
body:
## Purpose

This audit was commissioned ahead of the Foundation's decision to extend APD a full, exclusive licensing agreement. Its purpose was narrow: determine whether APD's proposed scope of work created jurisdictional conflict with any existing Foundation department, and if so, recommend either a revised scope or a denial of the license. This document is not an endorsement of APD as an organization; it addresses structure and jurisdiction only.

## Methodology

Representatives from the Records, Archives, and Information Security Administration (RAISA), the Internal Security Department (ISD), and the Intelligence Agency (IA) were each asked to review APD's proposed scope of work against their own department's mandate and flag any overlap. Findings below are organized by department.

## RAISA Findings

RAISA's mandate is Foundation-wide: it holds sole authority over classification policy, redaction standards, and the Foundation's core archival infrastructure, and it retains final say over what is preserved, what is destroyed, and what is shown to whom. No part of APD's proposed work touches any of this. APD was not offered, and did not request, access to RAISA's classification authority, its backup infrastructure, or its role in determining Foundation-wide archival policy.

What APD proposed instead was narrower and more mechanical: the engineering and maintenance of containment-monitoring hardware and predictive breach-detection software for a single overloaded cluster, Site-19 and its later satellite APD-14, work RAISA's own technical staff acknowledged they lacked the throughput to keep pace with. RAISA remains the policy authority for every system APD builds. APD builds; RAISA governs. The Board found no conflict, provided this boundary is enforced by contract and re-audited annually.

## Internal Security Department Findings

ISD's concern was narrower and more personal: could APD's presence introduce a security or loyalty risk inside the Foundation's own ranks. APD does not investigate Foundation personnel, does not have visibility into Foundation personnel files beyond what is strictly necessary to badge its own staff into shared facilities, and holds no enforcement or disciplinary authority over anyone, Foundation or APD.

ISD's recommendation was procedural rather than jurisdictional: that APD personnel be subject to the same vetting, monitoring, and incident-reporting standards as any other contractor with facility access, and that ISD retain the right to audit APD's own personnel records without notice. This was accepted as a condition of the license. The Board found no jurisdictional conflict; ISD's authority over Foundation security is undiminished, and APD was never proposed as an alternative to it.

## Intelligence Agency Findings

IA's mandate concerns the Foundation's posture toward the world outside its own walls: infiltration, counter-infiltration, and coordination with the Department of External Affairs. APD's work is entirely internal to Foundation-controlled facilities. It does not gather intelligence on other groups of interest, does not coordinate external operations, and has no contact with any organization outside the Foundation in the course of its contracted work. The Board found no conflict, and IA did not request further review.

## On Mobile Task Forces and Delta-Class Units

The most common objection raised internally was not jurisdictional but practical: if Mobile Task Forces already exist to recover anomalies exceeding standard field capacity, why does APD need field units of its own?

The answer is training, not authority. Foundation MTFs are drawn from across the Foundation's own personnel and are, by design, general-purpose: elite, but trained on Foundation equipment and Foundation procedure. None are trained on APD's proprietary detection and monitoring hardware, because that hardware did not exist until APD built it. Cross-training an existing MTF to APD's specification was assessed and rejected as slower and less reliable than simply allowing APD's own engineers, the people who built the equipment, to recover what it detects. APD's Delta-class units exist to close that specific and narrow gap. They hold no authority Foundation MTFs do not already have, they do not compete for the same assignments, and Foundation MTF Commanders retain the standing right to request their assistance, or override their involvement entirely, exactly as they may with any local field unit.

## Determination

The Board found no jurisdictional overlap sufficient to deny APD's license, provided the boundaries above are maintained: RAISA retains all classification and archival policy authority; ISD retains full audit and enforcement authority over APD personnel; IA's external mandate is untouched; and APD's Delta-class units operate strictly within the technical gap Foundation MTFs are not equipped to cover. The license was approved on this basis later that year.

## Addendum

This audit predates the events of June 2026 and the suspension of APD's license. It is preserved for the historical record. Whether the boundaries described above held in practice, particularly with respect to ISD's stated audit authority, is a question this document cannot answer.
